en.extract:
The white-tailed flycatcher (Leucoptilon concretum) is a species of bird in the family Muscicapidae. It is the only member of the monotypic genus Leucoptilon. It is found in submountain forests from the eastern Himalayas and northern Myanmar to southern China, western and southern Thailand, the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra and Borneo.


Taxonomy
The white-tailed flycatcher was formally described in 1836 by the German naturalist Salomon Müller based on a specimen collected in the interior of the west coast of Sumatra. Müller placed the species in the genus Muscicapa and coined the binomial name Muscicapa concreta. The specific epithet concretum is from Modern Latin concretus meaning "large", "sturdy" or "strong". It comes from Medieval Latin concretus meaning "concrete". The white-tailed flycatcher was previously assigned to the genus Cyornis but based on evidence from molecular phylogenetic studies the species is now placed in its own monotypic genus Leucoptilon that was introduced in 2021 by George Sangster and collaborators. The genus name combines the Ancient Greek λευκος/leukos meaning "white" with πτιλον/ptilon meaning "feather".
Three subspecies are recognised:

L. c. cyaneum (Hume, 1877) – northeast India to north Indochina and south Thailand
L. c. concretum (Müller, S, 1836) – Malay Peninsula and Sumatra
L. c. everetti (Sharpe, 1890) – Borneo
Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests and subtropical or tropical moist montane forests.

The '''white-tailed flycatcher''' ('''''Leucoptilon concretum''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Muscicapidae]]. It is the only member of the monotypic genus '''''Leucoptilon'''''. It is found in submountain forests from the eastern Himalayas and northern Myanmar to southern China, western and southern Thailand, the [[Malay Peninsula]], [[Sumatra]] and [[Borneo]].

==Taxonomy==
The white-tailed flycatcher was [[species description|formally described]] in 1836 by the German naturalist [[Salomon Müller]] based on a specimen collected in the interior of the west coast of Sumatra. Müller placed the species in the genus ''[[Muscicapa]]'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Muscicapa concreta''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Müller | first=Salomon  | author-link=Salomon Müller | date=1836 | title=Aanteekeningen, over de natuurlijke gesteldheid van een gedeelte der westkust en binnenlanden van Sumatra, met bijvoeging van eenige waarnemingen en beschrijvingen van verscheidene op dit, en andere Sunda-eilanden voorkomende dieren | language=Dutch | journal=Tijdschrift voor Natuurlijke Geschiedenis en Physiologie | volume=2 | pages=315–355 [351–352] | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DxkFAAAAQAAJ&pg=PA351 }}<!--BHL is missing vol 2--></ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1986 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=11 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | pages=360–361 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14484061 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''concretum'' is from [[Modern Latin]] ''concretus'' meaning "large", "sturdy" or "strong". It comes from [[Medieval Latin]] ''concretus'' meaning "concrete".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=concretus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=concretus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=1 September 2025 }}</ref> The white-tailed flycatcher was previously assigned to the genus ''[[Cyornis]]'' but based on evidence from [[molecular phylogenetic]] studies the species is now placed in its own [[monotypic]] genus ''Leucoptilon'' that was introduced in 2021 by [[George Sangster]] and collaborators.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Sangster|first1=G. |last2=Alström|first2=P. | last3=Gaudin | first3=J. | last4=Olsson | first4=U. |date=2021 |title=A new genus for the White-tailed Flycatcher ''Cyornis concretus'' (Aves: Muscicapidae) | journal=Zootaxa | volume=5072| issue=6 | pages=599–600 | doi=10.11646/zootaxa.5072.6.7 |pmid=35390843 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Chats, Old World flycatchers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/chats/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=1 September 2025}}</ref> The genus name combines the [[Ancient Greek]] λευκος/''leukos'' meaning "white" with πτιλον/''ptilon'' meaning "feather".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=Leucoptilon | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=Leucoptilon | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=1 September 2025 }}</ref>

Three [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''L. c. cyaneum'' ([[Allan Octavian Hume|Hume]], 1877) – northeast India to north Indochina and south Thailand
* ''L. c. concretum'' ([[Salomon Müller|Müller, S]], 1836) – [[Malay Peninsula]] and [[Sumatra]]
* ''L. c. everetti'' ([[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe]], 1890) – [[Borneo]]

Its natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s.
